title: Your Employees Don’t Need More Perks. They Need Work They Can Inhabit.
excerpt: Pay, purpose, flexibility, and wellness programs can improve the conditions around work. But engagement depends on whether the work itself gives employees a meaningful structure they can inhabit.
author: Ting Luo
body: Why employees disengage before they quit — and why pay, purpose, flexibility, and wellness programs are not enough.
The Engagement Paradox

Over the past decade, organizations have invested heavily in the standard responses to disengagement. They raised compensation. They introduced flexibility, remote and hybrid work, and experiments with four-day workweeks. They built wellness programs, mental health resources, and employee assistance platforms. They launched culture campaigns, purpose statements, and diversity initiatives. Many hired Chief People Officers to signal that employees matter.
It did not resolve the problem.

Gallup’s 2026 State of the Global Workplace report found that only 20% of employees worldwide were engaged in 2025, the lowest level since 2020. Gallup estimates that low engagement cost the global economy approximately $10 trillion in lost productivity. Deloitte’s 2023 research found that 68% of workers did not use the full value of the well-being resources their organizations offered because accessing those resources was too time-consuming, confusing, or cumbersome. McKinsey Health Institute has similarly argued that many employers focus on individual-level interventions that remediate burnout symptoms rather than resolving the organizational causes of burnout.
Something is being missed. And the missing layer is not another program.

The deeper problem is that most engagement strategies improve the conditions around work. They do not change the employee’s experience inside work. Employees may have better pay, more flexibility, and access to a meditation app — and still feel that the work itself gives them no meaningful place to exist inside the organization.

They disengage before they quit. They may remain in the role. They may perform the required tasks. But psychologically they have already withdrawn — because the work structure offers them output requirements, benefits, and slogans, but not a structure they can genuinely inhabit.
Compensation Is a Floor, Not a Home
None of this means that pay, purpose, flexibility, or wellness programs are useless. They are often necessary preconditions for engagement. But preconditions are not the same as causes.

Compensation is a floor, not a home. If pay is too low, employees cannot feel secure enough to care about the work. But once compensation reaches a livable threshold, it does not automatically create attachment, ownership, or meaning. Money can make a role tolerable. It cannot by itself make the workplace feel like a place where one’s presence genuinely matters.
Three mini-cases illustrate the progression.

The first is compensation and prestige. Jim Tang, a former Google software engineer who earned up to $326,000 annually, described originally imagining he would stay at Google for life. He eventually left — not because of poor pay or management failure, but because elite compensation and institutional prestige did not create a compelling reason to remain. The role did not offer a structure in which he could see a meaningful future. Google is not uniquely deficient; the point is that the deeper question — why should I bring myself fully here? — is one that pay and prestige alone cannot answer.

The second is purpose and mission. REI’s #OptOutside movement — closing its stores on Black Friday and giving thousands of employees a paid day to spend outside — is a genuinely powerful gesture. It aligns the company’s product with a lived value. But a 2025 National Employment Law Project report, drawing on interviews across ten REI stores, found that many workers experienced a gap between the co-op’s stated progressive values and their everyday reality of scheduling, voice, fairness, and recognition. Mission language becomes fragile when employees do not experience those values in the structure of the work itself. Purpose must be operational, not merely declared.

The third is flexibility and wellness. Hybrid work, four-day weeks, and well-being resources create room around work. They can reduce friction, stress, and logistical burden. But they do not necessarily create a reason to enter the work. When flexibility is offered as a stand-alone perk rather than embedded in a coherent work structure, employees may gain more time while still feeling that their presence inside the work does not particularly matter.
The pattern is consistent: pay is not enough, purpose is not enough, flexibility is not enough, and wellness programs are not enough. The missing layer is whether the work itself is habitable.
What Makes Work Habitable

A habitable workplace is not merely comfortable or generous. It is a structure in which employees can locate themselves meaningfully — where they can see how their judgment, care, memory, interpretation, and contribution enter the work. It gives them more than tasks to complete; it gives them a meaningful way to participate in a shared world.

Sustained engagement depends on whether the organization is designed so employees can experience agency, ownership, recognition, and meaningful contribution inside the structure of work. I call this existential value at work.

Existential value is the organizational experience that one’s presence matters as more than capacity to be deployed. It is not the same as feeling appreciated or receiving recognition awards. It is the structural experience of being a participant in something, rather than a resource consumed by it.

People do not necessarily resist effort. Many willingly invest energy in art, caregiving, community work, creative projects, and unpaid forms of contribution. What they resist is entering an organizational structure where their contribution is valued only as output. Modern organizations frequently ask employees to bring creativity, loyalty, emotional intelligence, and initiative, while simultaneously designing work as if people are primarily task executors. The result is not only burnout; it is psychological withdrawal from identification with the work.

A useful contrast comes from outside the corporate world. A Carousel for Missoula, a hand-carved community carousel in Montana, was built through more than 100,000 volunteer hours. This is not an argument that compensation does not matter. It shows why compensation cannot be the whole explanation for human effort. Money can secure time, labor, and role performance. But the quality of most work depends on something beyond role performance: the discretionary care, judgment, attention, and initiative that people choose to bring when they feel connected to what they are building. That is the difference between completing a task and making something excellent. People gave extraordinary effort to the carousel because the project allowed them to belong, build, leave an imprint, and participate in a shared civic world. The lesson for organizations is not to replace pay with purpose, but to recognize the limit of pay: it can buy function, but it cannot, by itself, create the deeper attachment that makes people bring judgment, care, initiative, and discretionary effort into the work.

Organizations cannot manufacture civic love by adding another perk. But they can learn from the underlying principle: people give more of themselves to structures that let them see themselves inside what is being built.
Why AI Makes This More Urgent
Generative AI and automation are intensifying the problem because they make the function-reduction logic of work more visible.

If an organization’s implicit message to employees is: your value lies in the functions you perform — writing, analyzing, summarizing, coding, designing, coordinating, deciding — then every new technology that can perform those functions makes employees feel more replaceable. The threat is not only economic. It is psychological. Employees lose the sense that their particular presence, judgment, and way of doing things matters to the organization.

Leaders often respond to AI integration with workforce planning: which roles will be automated, which will be augmented, how to retrain. These are legitimate questions. But they miss a prior question: what forms of human contribution remain meaningful when functions can be automated? Organizations that define people mainly by their functions will find that AI does not just threaten headcount — it erodes the psychological basis on which engaged employees experience themselves as valuable.

The answer is not to protect functions from automation. It is to build organizations where people are valued for more than functions: for their authorship, their participation in the work system, their care, their judgment, and their ability to hold complexity. In practical terms, this means redesigning roles so employees have a visible imprint on outcomes, a real voice in how work is done, and recognition for forms of judgment and care that metrics often miss.
Five Conditions of Habitable Work

What does it take to build a workplace people can genuinely inhabit? The following five conditions are not a checklist. They are the organizational design elements that determine whether work feels like participation or extraction.
1. Visible imprint / authorship

Employees need to see where their judgment, taste, care, or interpretation entered the work. This does not mean every person controls strategy. It means that somewhere in what the organization builds, an employee can point and say: that is where my thinking shaped the outcome.
Manager question: Where can employees see their own imprint in what the organization builds?
2. Participation in the work system

Employees need to experience the organization as a world they help shape, not merely a machine they serve. This can include involvement in team rituals, internal processes, project design, norms, and the structures that govern how the work is done.
Manager question: What parts of the team’s systems, rituals, or norms can employees meaningfully help shape?
3. Recognition beyond output

Many organizations recognize results while remaining blind to the invisible contributions that make results possible: emotional steadiness, mentoring, care, memory, repair, translation, noticing, and the capacity to hold complexity under pressure. These contributions are often essential. They rarely appear in performance metrics.
Manager question: What contributions are essential to how this team operates but invisible in current measurement systems?
4. Recovery and renewal

When organizations move from project to project without genuine recovery time, they treat people as renewable resources without actually renewing them. After intense work, employees need real space to recover, reflect, and re-enter the work with restored capacity. Without this, even meaningful work becomes extraction.
Manager question: How does the organization help people re-enter life after periods of intensive effort?
5. Shared care infrastructure

Organizational culture is not built primarily through values statements. It is built through the everyday operating conditions that determine whether employees feel supported, trusted, and held: the pacing of demands, the quality of mutual support, the visible presence of care from leadership, and the sense that the organization returns something to people rather than only consuming their energy.
Manager question: What operating conditions make employees feel that the organization is investing in their capacity to sustain the work, not only extracting from it?
The Hardest Part Is Not the Framework

The difficulty is not that these conditions are hard to understand. Most leaders already know that people want agency, recognition, recovery, and meaningful contribution. The harder problem is that building habitable work requires leaders to operate from a different assumption about people. If employees are seen primarily as risks to be controlled, organizations will keep designing surveillance, incentives, and compliance systems. If employees are seen as participants capable of care, judgment, and authorship, organizations can design structures that invite those capacities forward. The framework is practical, but it is not merely procedural. It asks leaders to decide what kind of human beings they believe they are managing.
The Design Question Leaders Should Be Asking

Employee engagement has been treated primarily as a motivation problem: how do we get people to try harder, care more, stay longer? The standard toolkit — compensation, perks, purpose campaigns, recognition platforms, wellness benefits — has been built around this assumption.

The evidence suggests the assumption is incomplete. Disengagement is not primarily a motivation failure. It is a design failure: organizations have structured work in ways that reduce people to task executors and then expressed surprise that people experience the work as hollow.
The reframe matters for leaders because it changes the question. Instead of asking how to motivate employees, leaders should ask:
Does the work we have designed give employees a meaningful way to participate in what we are building?
Do employees experience their own judgment, care, and contribution as visible inside this organization?
Are we building a workplace people can inhabit, or only conditions people can tolerate?

The future of employee engagement will not be solved by adding more programs around work. It will depend on whether leaders can redesign work itself as a structure people can inhabit — one in which employees experience agency, authorship, recognition, renewal, and a genuine reason to bring themselves fully into what they are building.
